figures.caption: Figure 7: Layer 3 per-dimension scores (0–25) for all seven models. Cinematographic Quality (CQ) exhibits the widest inter-model gap; Script Fidelity (SF) is the most compressed dimension.

Figure 9: Overview of the Agent System architecture. The main agent (Director) orchestrates preset specialist subagents for domain-specific tasks and interacts with the game engine via the MCP Cutscene Toolkit. A visual feedback loop enables perception-driven refinement through multimodal LLMs. After generation, a dynamically created custom subagent performs completeness and legality validation.

Figure 8: Per-scenario Call Completeness breakdown across CutsceneBench test scenarios (S1–S5). Performance generally decreases with task complexity, with the gap between top-performing and weaker models widening for complex scenarios.
Table 8: Combined evaluation results across all layers. L1–L2 values in %; L3 Total scored 0–100. Models are ordered by L3 Total.

Table 9: Call Completeness (%) by scenario tier. ∆ = S5 − S1. S1: single-character (20 cases), S2: two-person dialogue (20), S3: emotional scene (10), S4: three-person (10), S5: complex multi-turn (5).

Table 10: Camera Coverage (%) by scenario tier. ∆ = S5 − S1. Note: S5 sample sizes for MiniMax (2 cases) and S4 for MiniMax (6 cases) are smaller due to missing sessions, so those values have higher variance.
Table 1: CutsceneBench test scenarios. Complexity tiers are defined by the expected number of tool calls required for complete generation.
Table 5: Prompt element priority hierarchy for the Director Agent. Under context-window pressure, lower-priority elements are pruned first.
Table 2: Layer 1 evaluation results averaged over all test scenarios. All values in %.
Table 11: Layer 3 total score (0–100) by scenario tier, averaged over 5 videos per tier. ∆ = S5 − S1.
Table 13: Full L2 per-scenario results (%). TmpC = Temporal Consistency.
Table 12: Full L1 per-scenario results (%). MiniMax M2.5 has reduced case counts in S4 (6) and S5 (2) due to missing sessions.

Figure 4: CutsceneBench hierarchical evaluation architecture. Three layers progressively assess generation quality: L1 validates tool-call correctness and inter-tool dependency ordering, L2 programmatically inspects the final Level Sequence structure, and L3 evaluates narrative and cinematic quality via LLM-asJudge.
Table 3: Layer 2 evaluation results averaged over all test scenarios. All values in %.
Table 7: Position template summary.
Table 14: Full L3 per-scenario per-dimension results (scored 0–25; Tot. = total 0–100). Averaged over 5 videos per tier per model. TC = TmpCoh (abbreviated for table width).
Table 6: Summary of preset specialist subagents. Tool counts exclude query/perception tools shared across agents.

Figure 10: Hierarchical structure of the Level Sequence produced by S2_001. Two characters each carry audio, facial animation, and body animation tracks; five cameras provide continuous coverage via the camera-cut track.
Figure 3: Camera template library provided by the Cutscene Toolkit. See Appendix C for more details.

Table 4: Layer 3 evaluation results (LLM-as-Judge). Each dimension is scored 0–25; Total is 0–100. Scores are averaged over 25 sampled videos per model (5 per scenario tier). Judge model: Gemini 3.1 Pro.

Figure 6: Radar chart comparing model performance across L1 and L2 metrics of CutsceneBench. Each axis represents one evaluation metric (range 65–100%); values closer to the outer boundary indicate better performance. Camera Coverage (CamC) shows the widest inter-model spread.
Figure 2: Overview of the Cutscene Toolkit’s four functional modules and their MCP tool interfaces.

Figure 5: Inter-tool dependency DAG for the Cutscene Toolkit. Nodes are grouped by functional pipeline: Asset Query (blue), Character Pipeline (green), Audio Pipeline (orange), and Camera Pipeline (purple). Dashed edges denote precedence constraints (query before mutation); solid edges denote instance-level parameter dependencies. The DC metric verifies that the agent’s trajectory respects this ordering.

Figure 1: Cutscene Agent generates editable 3D cutscenes from natural-language scripts in minutes, producing multi-track Level Sequences with coordinated character animation, dialogue, and cinematography that remain fully editable by artists. Characters in the demo are taken from MetaHuman assets. See our project page for video results and more examples.
